segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [slowly] Chapter Twelve: Volume Four

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He was back in the locked room before the Civic Pitch finished its fourth bar.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The bells were still ringing their sequence across the city when he came down the stairs, past the kitchen where his father's teacup sat untouched on the table, and let himself in with the key he'd taken from the hook the previous afternoon and not returned. The three bells hung in their corners. The journals waited in their row.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He didn't touch the bells.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He pulled Volume Two from the shelf and sat on the floor with his back against the wall, because there was no chair, and opened it.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [pause]

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Volume Two was shorter than Volume One. The second-generation Bellwright — a man named Corvan, who had apparently been more interested in the practical work of bell maintenance than in his grandfather's theoretical obsessions — had recorded the births of his children, the replacement of two bells in the outer ring, a dispute with the Weavers' District over the pitch of the morning sequence, and almost nothing else. The handwriting was large and slightly careless. A practical man, Corvan. The kind of man who ate his breakfast and got on with things.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The entry about the question was on the last page before the binding changed to Volume Three.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [softly] Father's device. We do not speak of it.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass read it twice. Turned the page.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Volume Three was forty pages of bell maintenance, two pages of a dispute with the Academy over the licensing of a new resonance chamber in the Merchant Tier, and nothing else. No mention of the question at all. The third-generation Bellwright had apparently decided that even eight words was too many.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He set Volume Three aside and picked up Volume Four.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The handwriting changed immediately. Smaller, more careful, the letters formed with the precision of someone who had been trained to write for an audience. An Academy man, this Bellwright — Cass could tell from the first paragraph, where the author introduced himself as Aldric's great-great-grandson and a lecturer in acoustic theory at the Cantamura Academy of Civic Harmony. His name was Tomas. He had strong opinions about the relationship between interval and obligation, and he was not afraid to express them at length.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass read slowly. The argument was dense and he had to follow it carefully — Tomas had built it the way a good contract was built, each clause depending on the one before, the whole thing moving toward a conclusion that felt inevitable once you saw where it was going. The acoustic properties of the original bells. The specific alloy Aldric had used. The harmonic structure of the Civic Pitch, which Tomas had apparently spent three years measuring with equipment he'd built himself. The sub-harmonic. The question it asked.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [softly] Do you consent to be bound?

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Tomas had reached it by page thirty-one. He'd known exactly what it was. He'd laid out the implications in four careful paragraphs that Cass read with his jaw tight and his If the question required consent, and the question was built into every binding ratified against the morning Civic Pitch, then every binding ratified without genuine consent was technically —

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Page forty ended mid-sentence. The next page was blank. The forty-two after it were blank. The binding was still tight, the pages still cream-colored and flat, as if they'd been waiting this whole time.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass sat on the floor of the locked room and looked at the blank pages.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [slowly] Every binding ratified without consent. Every contract, every verdict, every obligation the city ran on — all of it technically void, technically always-void, going back two hundred years to the morning Aldric Bellwright struck the first Civic Pitch and asked a question the city had never answered.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass closed the journal. Set it on the floor beside him.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The three bells hung in their corners. The one on the left was slightly out of true, its mount tilted a fraction of a degree — he'd noticed it yesterday and noticed it again now,. The room was very quiet. The Civic Pitch had finished while he was reading.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He stayed on the floor for a while.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [pause]

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The Academy library opened at the second bell of morning and closed at the sixth bell of afternoon. Cass arrived at the third bell, paid his reader's fee with coins that rang at the right pitch against the testing plate, and went directly to the periodicals shelf.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The Voss paper was where it had always been: On the Persistence of Sub-harmonic Frequencies in Cast Bronze Instruments, by one A. Voss, published in the Academy's journal eleven years ago and apparently read by almost no one since, judging by the condition of the binding. Cass pulled it out and carried it to a table in the back corner, away from the two Academy students arguing over a contract theory text near the window.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He'd read this paper before. Twice, in fact — once in the early weeks of his investigation, when he'd been looking for any mention of the sub-harmonic, and once after he'd found the journals, when he understood better what to look for. He'd noted then that the argument stopped on page eleven.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He read it again now, with what he knew.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: A. Voss — Senna Voss, he was almost certain, though the paper used only the initial — had approached the problem from the other direction. Not from the journals, which she wouldn't have had access to, but from the acoustic measurements. She'd spent what must have been years taking careful readings of the morning Civic Pitch, using instruments she described in a footnote as

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: of non-standard design, calibrated to extend the measurable frequency range below the conventionally recognized lower threshold.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: She'd found the sub-harmonic. She'd characterized it precisely — frequency, interval relationship to the surface sequence, the specific harmonic structure that made it distinct from simple resonance artifacts.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Page ten was the measurement data. Page eleven was the beginning of the interpretation.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: It stopped six lines down.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [softly] The implications of this frequency, if its origin is intentional rather than incidental to the casting process, would require a systematic re-examination of —

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: And then nothing. The sentence ended without a period. The rest of the page was blank. The paper had been submitted and published in this state, with an editorial note at the front that described it as

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: a preliminary report, with further analysis forthcoming.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The further analysis had never appeared.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Voss had gotten there. Had understood what she was looking at. Had stopped writing.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass sat with the paper in his hands and felt the specific exhaustion of understanding something that didn't help him.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He put the paper back on the shelf.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The two Academy students were still arguing by the window. One of them was wrong about the resolution requirements for a minor-seventh codicil, and Cass could hear it in the precision of the other student's responses — too careful, too patient, the patience of someone who has decided not to correct directly. He walked past them and out into the corridor.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The Academy's east arcade was quiet at this hour. His footsteps carried further than they should have, bouncing off the limestone columns and coming back slightly delayed, slightly changed, the way sound always behaved in the spaces between buildings here. He counted the delay automatically. Roughly forty feet to the nearest reflective surface. He was doing it before he'd decided to.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He needed to talk to Torvald.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald Hess lived above a tally-song registry on the west edge of the Weavers' District, a fifteen-minute walk from the Academy. Cass had been there four times now and still got turned around on the last street, which looked like the second-to-last street until you noticed the blue-green stain on the doorstep of the corner building — Torvald's hands had left it there years ago, he'd explained once, when he used to carry the dye samples himself. The stain had never come out. He seemed to find this satisfying.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass found the stain and turned left.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald answered the door before Cass finished knocking, which meant he'd heard the footsteps on the stairs. He looked at Cass's face and stood aside.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Sit down,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: You look like you've been reading.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I have been reading.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: I mean the other kind.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald went to the small stove in the corner and lifted the kettle.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: You want tea?

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: No.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass sat in the wooden chair that had become, over four visits, his chair by default. The room smelled of old paper and the faint chemical sweetness of the district — dye-vats a few streets over, carried on the morning air.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I found what the blank pages were.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald set the kettle down. He turned and cupped his left ear slightly. He was paying close attention.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Tell me.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Volume Four. The fourth-generation Bellwright — he was an Academy lecturer, a theorist, spent years working through the acoustic implications of the sub-harmonic. The argument is good. It's — it's the same argument I've been building, but complete. Rigorous. He got all the way to the conclusion.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Which is.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: If the question requires consent, then any binding ratified without genuine consent has been technically void since the first morning Civic Pitch. Every contract. Every verdict. Every obligation the city runs on. Writing that down doesn't fix any of it. It just makes the whole structure fall at once.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald was quiet. He picked up his tea, which had been sitting on the table already, and drank from it.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: So he left it blank,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Tomas. The fourth-generation Bellwright. He got there and he left it blank. And the second generation got there before him and wrote eight words. And the third generation didn't write anything at all. And the Voss paper — I just re-read it, at the library. Voss got there too, from the acoustic measurements alone, without the journals. Stopped on page eleven.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: I know Senna Voss,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald said.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I thought it might be her.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: She doesn't talk about it.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He set the tea down.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: She hasn't talked about it in eleven years.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Because she made the same choice.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Because she made the same choice.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald sat down across from Cass, the chair creaking under his weight.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: What did you come here to tell me?

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: The bells contain something Aldric put there deliberately. Something that's been in the Civic Pitch for two hundred years. It's been suppressed — suppressed by every person who found it choosing the same thing. Silence. Because saying it out loud doesn't free anyone. It just breaks everything.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: You said this to your father?

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: My father's known for ten years. Since the night Perin signed.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald's expression didn't change. He'd suspected as much, probably — he was a man who built arguments from evidence, and the evidence of Eddan Bellwright's shaking hands had been available to anyone paying attention. He didn't ask how Cass knew. He trusted the ear.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: All right,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald said.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: What's it void?

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass looked at him.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Perin's contract,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald said.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: That's why you've been pulling this thread. Does it void Perin's contract?

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: It voids every contract,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald went still.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Not surprised, exactly. The stillness of a man absorbing a weight he'd been told to expect but hadn't quite believed until it landed. He sat with it for a moment. His stained hands rested on his knees. Outside, somewhere down the street, someone was hammering — a steady, ordinary sound that had nothing to do with any of this.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: That's not a solution,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald said.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: That's a different problem.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I know,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Your brother is still bound.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I know.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: And the people who've been coerced into bad contracts in the outer districts for sixty years are still bound. And the Weavers' District families who signed near the Fade Line without a broker are still bound. And voiding everything at once doesn't free any of them — it just creates a new fight that the people with the most resources win. Same people who've been winning.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Yes.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald picked up his tea again.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: So what do you want to do with it?

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald asked.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass opened his mouth. Closed it.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: [hesitantly] I don't know yet,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said, and it cost him something to say it, but it was the only true thing available.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald nodded, as if this were an acceptable answer. Maybe it was.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The knock at the door came before either of them spoke again. Torvald crossed the room and opened it. A boy stood on the landing — twelve, maybe thirteen, one of the Compact's runners, breathing like he'd taken the stairs fast. He held out a folded paper. Torvald took it, glanced at the seal, and held it toward Cass.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Addressed to the Bellwright family,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald said.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Runner was told to find you.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The seal was the House of Corda's — a tuning fork pressed into dark wax. Cass broke it.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The document was formal. It was exactly the kind of document you'd find in an Academy archive fifty years from now, properly composed, every clause in its correct position. He read it once fast and once slow.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [slowly] Maret Corda, head of the House of Corda, was invoking the co-custodial clause of an agreement dated twelve years prior between the Corda family and the Bellwright family. Under the terms of that clause, the House of Corda would assume shared authority over the forty-seven civic bells within thirty days unless the Bellwright family could demonstrate, to the satisfaction of a licensed Court Singer, that the six repaired bells in current service met the original custodianship standard established at the city's founding.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Thirty days.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The runner was still standing in the doorway. Cass looked up at him.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: How was this delivered? To my house?

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass asked.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He was trying to sound professional and mostly managing it.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Sung, sir,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: the runner said.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Formal messenger. Proper form. My master at the registry heard it go and sent me to find you.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass read the document a third time. The language was clean. The clauses held together. Every term was exactly what it claimed to be — he could feel the absence of an under-note as you feel the absence of pain when you're expecting it, a negative space that was almost more disorienting than the pain itself.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Maret believed every word of this. She wasn't lying. She was certain of her ground, certain enough to send a formal messenger to sing a notice in proper form to the Bellwright house, certain enough to start a thirty-day clock in front of the whole district.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The six repaired bells. He knew what his father knew about those bells. The old-alloy stock was running too low to replace them. The repairs would hold for three years, maybe, and his father had known that when he made them, and the custodianship standard required bells that held their tune without repair for —

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He folded the document.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Thank you,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said to the runner. The boy left.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald was watching him.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Thirty days,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: I heard.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: She's invoking the co-custodial clause. She wants shared authority over the forty-seven bells unless we can prove the repaired ones meet standard.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He set the document on the table between them.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: The repaired ones don't meet standard. My father knows it. She probably knows it too.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: So in thirty days, the House of Corda has a hand on the bells.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Yes.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald looked at the document. He touched it with two fingers, not picking it up, just touching the edge of it.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: And the bells set the Civic Pitch. And the Civic Pitch validates every contract ratified each day.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Yes.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: So in thirty days, if the Bellwright family can't prove the repairs hold —

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Maret Corda has a hand on the pitch,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: On every contract ratified in the city.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The hammering down the street had stopped. The room was quiet in the way rooms get when the thing you've been building toward has finally arrived and there's nothing left to do except sit with it.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass looked at the document. He looked at his own hands, which were steady —

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Thirty days to prove the repairs held. Thirty days before Maret Corda put her hand on the bells.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He had the knowledge that every Bellwright before him had chosen to bury. He had thirty days. He had a choice that none of the blank pages had been willing to make.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He picked up the document and put it in his coat.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I need to go home,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [pause]

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He didn't go home. Not right away.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He walked south through the Weavers' District, past the dye-vats and the shuttered looms, and then he kept walking, down toward the Fade Line where the careful speech of the inner city thinned into ordinary noise. The afternoon was cold. His coat was thin. He walked until his legs ached and the Bell Tower was small behind him, just a shape above the rooftops, and then he stood at the Fade Line and listened to the sound of a city that didn't know what he knew.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: A woman was arguing with a vendor about the price of candles. Two children ran past him, shouting. A dog barked from a doorway. Ordinary sounds, unmanaged, the kind of noise people made when they weren't afraid of being heard.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [slowly] Every contract ratified today was void. Every one ratified yesterday. Every one the city had ever sung into the air between parties who believed they were being bound.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The woman bought her candles. The children disappeared around a corner. The dog lost interest.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He turned back toward the inner city. The walk home took forty minutes, and he used every one of them, not thinking — not making plans, not running through options, not doing the arithmetic of thirty days — just walking, carrying the weight of what he'd read in the locked room, letting his body do the work of moving through space while the knowledge settled into him the way a bell's tone settled into stone.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: By the time he reached the Bell Quarter the evening bells were ringing from the outer districts. The workshop light was on. He could see his father's shadow through the glass.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He didn't go in. He went upstairs, lay on his bed in the dark, and stared at the ceiling until morning.
